title: Enhancement of superconducting stiffness in hybrid superconducting-metallic bilayers
authors: J. E. Ebot, Lorenzo Pizzino, Sam Mardazad, Johannes S. Hofmann, Thierry Giamarchi, Adrian Kantian
abstract: Boosting superconductivity by metallic reservoirs is the essence of Kivelson's bilayer proposal.   One layer provides pairing to the electrons, while the weakly coupled metal provides additional phase coherence to those pairs by mediating extended-range pair-pair coupling.   Demonstrating significant and unambiguous performance gains with strong-coupling methods for such set-ups had been difficult.   In the present work, we study these systems doped away from half-filling, corresponding to a partially spin-polarized 1D Anderson- or Kondo-lattice.   We show that this breaks the coexistence of dominant superconducting and density-density correlations decisively in favour or the former.   Consequently, we provide evidence that in this doped regime, superconducting near-long-range order is not precluded by a small charge-gap in the thermodynamic limit, as we have recently shown to be the case at half-filling [JE Ebot $et$ $al.$, arXiv:2602.11153 [cond-mat.supr-con]].   We study the complex manner in which the enhancement of superconductivity in the pairing layer depends on the parameters of the metal, and especially that both pairing-limited and stiffness-limited regimes may appear in these systems.   In addition to superconducting bilayers, our results are relevant, via a particle-hole transformation, for heavy-fermion Kondo-lattice materials in magnetic fields, as we provide previously lacking insight on the competition between antiferromagnetic and easy-plane magnetism, as well as a route for comprehensive indirect tests of Kivelson's bilayer proposal well beyond previous capabilities.
summary: This paper demonstrates that doping a superconducting-metallic bilayer away from half-filling enhances superconducting correlations by breaking the competition with density-density correlations. It identifies distinct regimes of superconductivity controlled by pairing amplitude and phase stiffness. The findings also provide a theoretical link to understanding magnetic ordering in heavy-fermion Kondo lattices.
main_problem: The study investigates how a metallic reservoir can enhance superconducting correlations in a paired subsystem, specifically addressing whether doping the system away from half-filling can overcome the suppression of long-range order caused by a charge gap.
main_result: Doping away from half-filling breaks the degeneracy between superconducting and density-density correlations in favor of superconductivity, allowing for near-long-range order and revealing a transition between pairing-limited and stiffness-limited regimes.
method: The authors employ modern many-body numerical techniques, specifically Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) and Auxiliary-Field Quantum Monte Carlo (AFQMC), alongside analytical Conformal Field Theory (CFT) approaches.
model_or_system: A 1D hybrid bilayer model consisting of a pairing subsystem (P) with negative-U sites coupled via tunneling to a metallic subsystem (M). The system can be mapped to a 1D Anderson or Kondo lattice via particle-hole transformation.
key_observables: S-wave pair-pair correlations, density-density correlations, single-particle correlations, Luttinger liquid parameters (Kp-1 and Km-1), and superconducting susceptibility.
important_parameters: Interaction strength (U), inter-subsystem tunneling (t-perp), metallic kinetic energy (tm), and electron densities (np, nm).
main_assumptions: The study focuses on 1D systems; extensions to 2D and 3D rely on theoretical scaling arguments and known physics of heavy-fermion materials.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the two-chain setup of the P-subsystem and M-subsystem and the mapping to the Anderson/Kondo lattice.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Kivelson bilayer proposal, defines the 1D Hamiltonian and its mapping to magnetic models, presents numerical results for various doping and coupling regimes, and discusses the implications for heavy-fermion materials.
why_interesting: The paper provides deep insights into how engineered coupling between different quantum subsystems can manipulate many-body correlations and phase stiffness, which is highly relevant to the control of correlations in ultracold atomic gases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic representation of the Hamiltonian in Eq. (1). It comprises of a pairing subsystem (“P-subsystem”) with on-site pairing U (and chemical potential µm, when performing AFQMC simulations, which are in the grand- canonical regime), and a metallic subsystem (M-subsystem; we will use ‘metal’ interchangeably) with nearest-neighbour tunneling tm and chemical potential term µm. Coupling be- tween the subsystems is provided by tunneling with amplitude t⊥.

FIG. 2. Cλ(i, j) (open markers) and |Nλ(i, j)| (shaded mark- ers) of (a) P-subsystem and (b) M-subsystem in regime 3 with L = 100 and np = 0.38, nm = 0.78. Cλ(i, j) has infinite correlation length and |Np(i, j)| is suppressed. Cλ(i, j) (open markers: , , ) and |Nm(i, j)| (shaded markers: , , ). Dashed lines are the power-law fit of Cλ, with fitting param- eters K−1 p and AC,p.
